sourceMovie.title: Felon
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Felon (2008) stuck because it commits to its crime premise without apologizing—viewers remember set pieces and momentum as much as plot. Ric Roman Waugh's control of tone keeps the ride coherent. The crime and drama blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Faster
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Faster (2010) belongs to the same conversation as Felon — both anchor their crime in drama emotion that doesn't let go. The dark undercurrent connects them more than any plot summary would suggest.

recommendations.title: Lock Up
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Felon's crime stayed with you, Lock Up (1989) delivers the same sensation through an entirely different story. The drama through-line is the real common thread between them.

recommendations.title: Undisputed II: Last Man Standing
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Where Felon excels in crime, Undisputed II: Last Man Standing (2006) doubles down on drama to comparable effect. The tonal similarities run deep enough that watching both back-to-back feels intentional.

recommendations.title: We Die Young
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: We Die Young (2019) is the natural next step after Felon: same crime DNA, different perspective. The dark streak and drama elevate it well above a simple genre exercise.

recommendations.title: Léon: The Professional
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Fans of Felon who respond to its crime will find Léon: The Professional (1994) immediately familiar. Both films share a drama sensibility without ever feeling like carbon copies of each other.

recommendations.title: Lucky Number Slevin
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: There's a reason Felon fans keep landing on Lucky Number Slevin (2006) — the crime is unmistakably related even when the plots diverge. Both films operate on the same emotional frequency, especially in their third acts.

recommendations.title: A History of Violence
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: A History of Violence draws from the same crime well as Felon, channeling drama in ways that feel unmistakably related. The 2005 film makes a natural companion watch — similar in spirit but distinctly its own.

recommendations.title: Shot Caller
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Shot Caller (2017) captures the crime appeal of Felon without simply imitating it. The drama streak running through both films is what puts Shot Caller at the top of any "if you liked…" shortlist.

recommendations.title: Bound by Honor
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The crime appeal of Felon — that dark edge, the drama — translates cleanly into Bound by Honor (1993). Different setting, same essential satisfaction.

recommendations.title: Copshop
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The filmmakers behind Copshop (2021) clearly understood what makes crime work — the same quality that keeps Felon rewatchable. Expect the same dark rhythm, executed with a fresh angle.

faq.question: What should I watch after Felon?
faq.answer: Try Faster, Lock Up, Undisputed II: Last Man Standing—each shares DNA with Felon while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Felon?
faq.answer: Felon (2008) is best described as Crime, Drama, Thriller, Action. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Felon worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 7.1/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this crime lane.
